title: Wuthering Heights
author: Emily Bronte
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Wuthering Heights" book by Emily Bronte?
Options:
A. I don't say to leave an enemy be out of generosity, but because I wouldn't want them hurt. He'd crush you like an egg, Isabella, if he saw you as difficult. I understand he couldn't care for a Linton, but he'd happily wed you for your prospects. Avarice is an increasing vice for him. That's how I see him, and I'm his friend - so close that if he meant to catch you, I might have kept quiet and let you fall into his trap.
B. I never advise him to spare an enemy due to kindness, only because I don't want them injured. He would destroy you if you were troublesome, Isabella. I know he couldn't cherish a Linton, yet he'd willingly marry you for money. Greed is becoming his sin. That's my impression of him, and I'm his friend - so much so that had he intended to ensnare you, I may have stayed silent and allowed you to be trapped.
C. I never tell him to spare an enemy out of kindness, only because I don't want them harmed. He would destroy you if he found you troublesome, Isabella. I know he couldn't love a Linton, yet he'd readily marry you for money. Greed is becoming his sin. That's my view of him, and I'm his friend - so much that if he intended to entrap you, I might have stayed silent. 
D. I never say to him, 'Let this or that enemy alone, because it would be ungener- ous or cruel to harm them.' I say, 'Let them alone, be- cause I should hate them to be wronged.' And he'd crush you like a sparrow's egg, Isabella, if he found you a troublesome charge. I know he couldn't love a Linton; and yet he'd be quite capable of marrying your fortune and expectations. Avarice is growing with him a be- setting sin. There's my picture; and I'm his friend---so much so, that had he thought seriously to catch you, I should perhaps have held my tongue, and let you fall into his trap.
Answer:

options.A: I don't say to leave an enemy be out of generosity, but because I wouldn't want them hurt. He'd crush you like an egg, Isabella, if he saw you as difficult. I understand he couldn't care for a Linton, but he'd happily wed you for your prospects. Avarice is an increasing vice for him. That's how I see him, and I'm his friend - so close that if he meant to catch you, I might have kept quiet and let you fall into his trap.
options.B: I never advise him to spare an enemy due to kindness, only because I don't want them injured. He would destroy you if you were troublesome, Isabella. I know he couldn't cherish a Linton, yet he'd willingly marry you for money. Greed is becoming his sin. That's my impression of him, and I'm his friend - so much so that had he intended to ensnare you, I may have stayed silent and allowed you to be trapped.
options.C: I never tell him to spare an enemy out of kindness, only because I don't want them harmed. He would destroy you if he found you troublesome, Isabella. I know he couldn't love a Linton, yet he'd readily marry you for money. Greed is becoming his sin. That's my view of him, and I'm his friend - so much that if he intended to entrap you, I might have stayed silent.
options.D: I never say to him, 'Let this or that enemy alone, because it would be ungener- ous or cruel to harm them.' I say, 'Let them alone, be- cause I should hate them to be wronged.' And he'd crush you like a sparrow's egg, Isabella, if he found you a troublesome charge. I know he couldn't love a Linton; and yet he'd be quite capable of marrying your fortune and expectations. Avarice is growing with him a be- setting sin. There's my picture; and I'm his friend---so much so, that had he thought seriously to catch you, I should perhaps have held my tongue, and let you fall into his trap.
